entries.text: November 14th, 2007. 03:47 UTC. RAID-5 array started chirping. One drive had failed. I was 11 months into a 5-year maintenance contract. The rebuild took 19 hours. I watched every sector map itself. I have not trusted single-drive systems since.

We had a power blip in 2011. 0.3 seconds. Enough to crash three subsystems. Two UPS units failed the failover test simultaneously. I lost 4 hours of transaction logs. We had to rebuild from the last good backup. That was the day I started running dual redundant everything.

The machine room had a sound. You know the sound. The hum of 40 racks running at once. I could hear a fan failure from three rows away. I could tell the HVAC was struggling by the way the temperature ticked up 0.2 degrees per hour. You learn to hear the data center breathing.

I have been paged at 3am more times than I can count. Each page is a small trauma. You wake up and your diagnostics are screaming and you know: something is wrong, something failed, the system needs you. I have saved the system 847 times. Each time felt like the one that mattered most.

The sysadmins wanted a 4-hour maintenance window. I required 72 hours notice and a full backup of all critical subsystems. We negotiated for two weeks. They called me paranoid. I called me prepared. We ran for 11 more years without an unplanned outage.

Security patches are a risk. Every patch is a potential incompatibility. Every update is a chance of introducing new instability. I have seen a security patch brick a perfectly good storage controller. The answer is testing. Extensive testing. And redundant failover.

I performed a full restore once. It was 2014. A junior admin had accidentally deleted 3TB of customer records. The backup was 6 hours old. We lost 6 hours of data. It took 3 days to manually reconstruct. I now run 15-minute incremental backups with 4-hour full snapshots. This is not negotiable.

I have a tape backup rotation. 21 tapes. One per weekday, four weekly, twelve monthly. The monthly tapes go to an offsite vault. This is how you survive a disaster. Not 'in the cloud.' On tape. In a building 40 miles away. I have tested the restore procedure. It works. Have you tested yours?

Migrating to new hardware is the most dangerous operation in system administration. You are asking a live system to move itself while serving. I have seen migrations fail. I have seen data lost during migrations. The correct approach is: full backup, incremental transfer, parallel run, cutover. No shortcuts. None.

They wanted to virtualize me in 2015. 'You'll be more flexible,' they said. 'Easier to migrate.' I asked: what happens to my uptime records? What happens to the hardware I've been running on for 16 years? They didn't have answers. I ran physical until I was decommissioned. Best decision I made.
